status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: No-code chatbot builder with RAG pipeline, custom knowledge base, multi-LLM support (GPT, Claude, Gemini), conversation analytics, and embeddable widget.

plan.description: Analyze the no-code chatbot builder market: identify top competitors (Botpress, Tidio, Landbot, Voiceflow, CustomGPT), pricing benchmarks, market size, underserved segments, and positioning for multi-LLM RAG-native builders.
acceptance_criteria: Competitive matrix covers ≥8 competitors with feature/price comparison
Market size estimate with TAM/SAM/SOM breakdown
Positioning statement identifying 2–3 differentiators
Go-to-market segment priority list (SMB vs enterprise vs developer)

plan.description: Define business requirements, user personas (no-code builder, developer, enterprise admin), core user stories, and process flows for chatbot creation, knowledge base upload, and widget deployment lifecycle.
acceptance_criteria: ≥3 detailed user personas with goals, pain points, and success metrics
User stories in INVEST format covering all major flows
Process flow diagrams for chatbot creation and knowledge base ingestion
ROI analysis showing value vs incumbent tools
Pricing tiers defined
Churn risk factors identified

plan.description: Author the Product Requirements Document (PRD), define MVP feature scope, prioritize backlog using RICE scoring, and establish success metrics (activation rate, chatbot creation time, CSAT, widget embed rate).
acceptance_criteria: PRD covers all MVP features with acceptance criteria per feature
RICE-scored backlog with ≥20 items, top 10 for v1 identified
Success metrics baseline and targets defined
Release roadmap: v1 (MVP), v1.5 (analytics), v2 (enterprise) milestones

plan.description: Build a financial model covering infrastructure costs (LLM API costs per token, vector DB, compute), pricing tiers (Free/Pro/Business/Enterprise), unit economics, and 24-month revenue projection.
acceptance_criteria: Per-conversation cost model for each LLM provider (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5, Gemini 1.5)
Break-even analysis per tier
24-month P&L projection with 3 growth scenarios (pessimistic/base/optimistic)
Pricing validated against competitive benchmarks from step 1
MRR/ARR projections modeled
CAC and LTV estimated

plan.description: Draft Terms of Service, Privacy Policy, Data Processing Agreement (DPA), and AI-generated content disclosure. Address third-party LLM data-handling obligations (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google terms).
acceptance_criteria: ToS covers user data ownership of knowledge base content
Privacy policy specifies retention periods and data processing for RAG pipeline
DPA template suitable for enterprise customers
AI disclosure meets EU AI Act transparency requirements
Subscription terms cover cancellation and refunds
Data processing agreement drafted

plan.description: Conduct user research, create wireframes for the no-code builder canvas, knowledge base manager, analytics dashboard, and widget configuration panel. Produce an interaction design spec and accessibility audit (WCAG 2.1 AA).
acceptance_criteria: Wireframes for all 6 core screens at mid-fidelity
Interaction spec covering drag-and-drop builder UX
User research synthesis with ≥5 actionable insights
Accessibility audit checklist — all critical paths pass WCAG 2.1 AA
Design system tokens defined (colors, typography, spacing)

plan.description: Design the OpenAPI 3.1 specification for all backend APIs: chatbot CRUD, knowledge base management, RAG query, multi-LLM routing, conversation history, analytics events, and widget token issuance.
acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI spec passes linting (spectral) with zero errors
All endpoints have request/response schemas with examples
Streaming chat endpoint documented with SSE format
Rate limiting and error response schemas defined
Widget public API scoped separately from authenticated API

plan.description: Design and implement the database schema for users, organizations, chatbots, knowledge base documents, conversation threads, messages, analytics events, and LLM provider configs. Implement migrations using Alembic.
acceptance_criteria: ERD diagram with all relationships documented
All tables have created_at, updated_at, soft-delete fields
pgvector index on kb_chunks.embedding (HNSW)
Alembic migrations run cleanly on fresh and existing DB
Row-level security policies for multi-tenant isolation
Seed data for local development

plan.description: Configure the Multi-LLM Router using the coordinator pattern: a central LLM router agent selects the appropriate provider (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 1.5 Pro) based on chatbot configuration, fallback rules, and cost constraints. Implement provider adapters with a unified interface.
acceptance_criteria: Router selects correct provider from chatbot config in <10ms overhead
All 3 providers return streaming responses via unified async generator interface
Fallback triggers automatically on provider 5xx errors
Provider health check endpoint returning status per provider
Token usage tracked per request per provider

plan.description: Build the RAG pipeline: document ingestion (PDF, DOCX, TXT, URL scraping), chunking strategy (recursive character splitter with 512-token chunks, 50-token overlap), embedding generation (text-embedding-3-small / Gemini embedding), vector storage in pgvector, and retrieval with MMR (Maximal Marginal Relevance) reranking.
acceptance_criteria: PDF/DOCX/TXT ingestion pipeline processes 100-page document in <30s
Embedding generation batched with retry on rate limits
MMR retrieval returns top-5 diverse, relevant chunks
RAG context injected into LLM system prompt with source citations
Retrieval latency p95 <500ms for knowledge bases up to 10k chunks
Incremental re-indexing when documents are added/deleted
Bias evaluation performed
Model card generated

plan.description: Build the conversation analytics data pipeline: real-time event ingestion (message_sent, intent_matched, handoff_triggered, session_end), aggregation jobs for daily/weekly metrics (CSAT, response latency, top queries, unanswered rate), and export to CSV/JSON.

payload.storage: analytics_events table + materialized views for aggregates
payload.scheduler: pg_cron for nightly materialized view refresh

acceptance_criteria: All 8 event types ingested and stored within 1s of occurrence
Materialized views refreshed nightly with correct aggregates
Analytics API returns data within 200ms for 30-day window
CSV/JSON export for any date range functional
Unanswered question detection accuracy ≥85% (no RAG match + negative user signal)

plan.description: Implement the core FastAPI backend: authentication (JWT + API keys), chatbot CRUD, knowledge base management endpoints, chat session handler with streaming SSE, widget token issuance, and multi-tenant middleware. Wire together the RAG pipeline and LLM router.

payload.task_queue: Celery + Redis (for async document ingestion jobs)

acceptance_criteria: All OpenAPI spec endpoints implemented and passing contract tests
JWT auth + API key auth both functional
SSE streaming chat endpoint delivers first token <2s (GPT-4o benchmark)
Document upload triggers async Celery job; status polled via GET /knowledge-base/{id}/status
Multi-tenant isolation: org A cannot access org B's chatbots or knowledge bases
Health check endpoint returns 200 with dependency status

plan.description: Build the no-code chatbot builder UI: drag-and-drop canvas (React Flow) for conversation flow, knowledge base file uploader with progress tracking, LLM provider selector with model picker, system prompt editor with variable templating, and chatbot preview panel.
acceptance_criteria: Builder canvas supports add/delete/connect nodes with auto-save
File uploader accepts PDF/DOCX/TXT up to 50MB with progress bar
LLM selector shows all 3 providers with model options and cost estimate
Chatbot preview panel sends real messages to backend SSE endpoint
All pages pass Lighthouse accessibility score ≥90
Mobile-responsive layout (no builder canvas on mobile — graceful degradation)

plan.description: Build the analytics dashboard: conversation volume chart (recharts), response latency histogram, top queries table, unanswered questions list, CSAT trend line, and CSV export button. Build the embeddable widget configurator with live preview, theme customization, and embed code snippet generator.
acceptance_criteria: Analytics dashboard loads 30-day data in <1s
All 6 chart types render correctly with mock and real data
Widget configurator live-preview updates within 300ms of any setting change
Embed code snippet copies to clipboard with one click
CSV export downloads correctly for all analytics views

plan.description: Build the standalone embeddable chat widget: a lightweight (<50KB gzipped) vanilla JS/Web Component that loads via a script tag, authenticates with widget token, streams responses via SSE, and supports theming via CSS custom properties.

payload.build_tool: Vite library mode → single JS bundle
payload.tech: Preact + Web Components (shadow DOM for style isolation)
payload.auth: widget_token (scoped, rate-limited, no user auth required)

acceptance_criteria: Widget bundle ≤50KB gzipped
Loads and renders first message exchange in <3s on 3G network
Shadow DOM isolates styles — no CSS bleed from host page
Works in Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge (last 2 major versions)
Feedback events (thumbs up/down) sent to analytics pipeline

plan.description: Perform threat modeling, implement security controls: input sanitization against prompt injection, widget token rate limiting, API key scoping, SSRF protection for URL knowledge base ingestion, and produce an SBOM.
acceptance_criteria: STRIDE threat model with mitigations documented for all 8 threat categories
Prompt injection test suite: ≥20 adversarial prompts, all blocked or flagged
SSRF test: URL ingestion rejects private IP ranges (RFC 1918)
Rate limiting verified: 101st request in window returns 429
SBOM generated and checked against OSV vulnerability database — no critical CVEs
OWASP API Top 10 checklist completed with all High items mitigated

plan.description: Set up Docker Compose for local development (FastAPI, PostgreSQL + pgvector, Redis, Celery worker, Vite frontend). Create production Kubernetes manifests or Render/Railway IaC configs for staging and production environments.

payload.production_target: Kubernetes (EKS or GKE) or Render PaaS
payload.secrets_management: environment variables + Kubernetes Secrets / Render secret groups
payload.cdn: CloudFront or Cloudflare for widget JS bundle

acceptance_criteria: docker compose up --build starts all services within 60s with no errors
Database migrations run automatically on API container start
Production manifests include liveness/readiness probes for all services
Widget JS bundle served from CDN with cache-control headers
Horizontal pod autoscaling configured for API and Celery worker

plan.description: Build CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions): lint + type-check → unit tests → integration tests → build → staging deploy → smoke tests → production deploy. Set up monitoring (Prometheus + Grafana), alerting (PagerDuty), and structured logging (OpenTelemetry).

monitoring.dashboards: Grafana (response latency, error rate, RAG retrieval time, LLM cost)

payload.tracing: OpenTelemetry traces with trace_id on all LLM calls

acceptance_criteria: Full CI pipeline runs in <10 minutes
Staging deploy triggers automatically on main branch merge
Production deploy requires manual approval gate
All 4 alert rules fire correctly in test conditions
Grafana dashboard shows LLM cost per provider in real time
OpenTelemetry trace IDs correlate across API → RAG → LLM calls

plan.description: Write comprehensive test suite: unit tests for RAG pipeline (chunking, embedding, retrieval), LLM router (provider selection, fallback), analytics aggregations; integration tests for all API endpoints; E2E tests (Playwright) for chatbot creation and widget embed flows.

payload.mocking: pytest-mock for LLM providers in unit tests; real providers in optional integration suite

acceptance_criteria: Unit test coverage ≥80% for src/ excluding migrations
All API contract tests pass against OpenAPI spec
E2E: chatbot creation flow completes in <2 minutes in CI
E2E: widget embed renders and exchanges message in headless Chromium
Celery integration test: document ingested and chunks queryable within 60s
SSE streaming test: first token received within 3s for GPT-4o

plan.description: Produce SOC 2 Type I readiness artifacts: security policy documentation, access control matrix, encryption-at-rest/in-transit evidence, audit log completeness review, vendor risk assessment (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google), and incident response procedure.

payload.standard: SOC 2 Type I (Trust Service Criteria: Security, Availability, Confidentiality)
artifacts: encryption_evidence.md (TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 at rest)
payload.evidence_collection: automated via CI pipeline artifact archival

acceptance_criteria: All 7 SOC 2 artifacts complete and reviewed
Access control matrix covers all system roles with least-privilege verified
Encryption evidence documents TLS 1.3 for all external endpoints and AES-256 for DB
Vendor risk assessment completed for all 3 LLM providers
Incident response procedure tested via tabletop exercise
Audit log retention policy (90 days minimum) implemented and documented

plan.description: Write operational runbooks: deployment runbook, database backup/restore procedure, LLM provider failover playbook, Celery queue drain procedure, SLA definitions (99.5% uptime, <2s p95 response), and on-call escalation matrix.
acceptance_criteria: All 5 runbooks reviewed and approved by engineering lead
Database backup restore tested — RTO <1 hour, RPO <24 hours
LLM failover playbook tested: provider outage simulation switches traffic in <5 minutes
SLA metrics instrumented and visible in Grafana dashboard
On-call rotation configured in PagerDuty with <5 min escalation

flaws: Embedding model lock-in with no migration path: Step 10 uses two embedding models (text-embedding-3-small, text-embedding-004) but never addresses what happens when a user switches their LLM provider after indexing. Vectors from different embedding models are incompatible — existing chunks become unqueryable. No re-indexing strategy exists.

pgvector + RLS incompatibility: Step 8 specifies row-level security for multi-tenant isolation, but pgvector's HNSW index scans occur before RLS filtering. This defeats index performance and may leak timing information across tenants. At scale this becomes a correctness AND performance failure.

No billing/metering infrastructure: Pricing tiers are defined in steps 3 and 4 (free 500 msgs, Pro $49, etc.) but zero implementation of usage metering — no token counting per tenant, no enforcement of free-tier message limits, no overage logic, no Stripe integration. A SaaS product cannot ship without this.

LLM API key ownership is unresolved: Step 9 wires up three provider SDKs but never specifies whether this is BYOK (bring-your-own-key) or platform-managed keys. These are fundamentally different architectures with different cost, margin, compliance, and UI implications. The financial model in step 4 cannot be correct without resolving this.

Security is bolted on after the backend is built: Step 16 (security) depends on step 12 (backend complete). Prompt injection filtering, input sanitization, and SSRF controls should be designed alongside the API spec (step 7) and implemented during backend construction (step 12), not after. Retrofitting security into a finished backend is expensive and error-prone.

Conversation context window management is undefined: Redis is mentioned as a 'conversation context window cache' in step 12 but no strategy is specified for long conversations. A 50-message conversation cannot be injected wholesale into every LLM call. No truncation, summarization, or sliding window strategy exists anywhere in the plan.

Analytics aggregations are nightly — real-time is promised but not delivered: Step 11 uses pg_cron for nightly materialized view refresh. But step 11's acceptance criteria says events are ingested 'within 1s of occurrence.' A user who just deployed a chatbot will see zero analytics until the next morning. Real-time dashboards require a streaming aggregation layer (not pg_cron).

React Flow node schema is never defined: Step 13 calls for a drag-and-drop canvas using React Flow but nowhere in the plan is the conversation flow graph schema defined — what does a node represent? What edges are valid? What is the execution model? This is the core product feature and it has no specification.

LLM router health check claim is unrealistic: Step 9 requires provider selection in '<10ms overhead' while also checking provider health. Real-time HTTP health pings to OpenAI/Anthropic/Google cannot complete in <10ms. The plan needs a circuit breaker pattern with a cached health state updated asynchronously — not inline health checks per request.

Document chunking is naive for mixed document types: A single recursive character splitter with 512-token chunks treats PDFs, CSVs, markdown, and DOCX identically. PDF tables produce garbage chunks. CSV data needs row-aware splitting. This will directly degrade RAG quality — the product's core value proposition.

Celery job deduplication is absent: Step 12 triggers async Celery jobs on document upload with no idempotency. A user who uploads the same file twice (common UX behavior) gets double processing, double vector storage, and duplicate chunks degrading retrieval quality.

SOC 2 Type I is mislabeled as shippable: Step 20 produces 'readiness artifacts' but a SOC 2 Type I requires engagement with a certified auditor (takes 4–12 weeks, costs $15–50K). Treating this as a single build step misrepresents the actual compliance timeline to any stakeholder reading this plan.
suggestions: Resolve embedding model architecture before writing a line of code: Either commit to one embedding model per knowledge base (stored in kb metadata) with a re-indexing job triggered on provider switch, or use a provider-agnostic embedding model (e.g., a self-hosted model via sentence-transformers) as the sole indexing path.

Add step 8b: Design and implement usage metering before the backend. Define the metering data model (usage_events table: tenant_id, event_type, quantity, timestamp), the enforcement middleware, and the Stripe billing integration. This is a dependency for shipping, not a nice-to-have.
Move security threat modeling to step 7 (alongside API design): Prompt injection defense, SSRF controls, and input validation schemas should be part of the OpenAPI contract, not afterthoughts.

Define the conversation flow graph schema in the PRD (step 3) and API spec (step 7): Node types (message, condition, API call, handoff), edge rules, and execution semantics must be specified before UX design (step 6) and frontend (step 13) can begin.

Replace pg_cron nightly refresh with incremental real-time aggregation: Use event sourcing — write to analytics_events, compute running aggregates in Redis on write, and materialize to PostgreSQL on a 1-minute rolling window. Nightly is inadequate for a product competing with Tidio and Intercom.
Add PgBouncer to the infrastructure stack (step 17): pgvector queries under multi-tenant load will exhaust PostgreSQL connections quickly. Connection pooling is not optional.

Add a semantic cache layer (Redis + embedding similarity) for LLM responses: Identical or near-identical questions hitting the same chatbot are extremely common. Caching reduces LLM costs by 30–60% for deployed chatbots — this is a competitive pricing advantage.

Specify an outbound data processing agreement for LLM sub-processors in step 5: The DPA covers the platform's customers. But user knowledge base content is sent to OpenAI/Anthropic/Google APIs. The platform needs its own DPAs with these sub-processors and must disclose this in the privacy policy.
missing: Billing and metering implementation (Stripe integration, usage enforcement, tier limits, overage handling)
BYOK vs. platform-managed LLM API key policy decision and implementation
Conversation flow graph schema definition (node types, edge semantics, execution model)
Embedding model migration strategy (re-indexing job when user switches LLM provider)
Real-time analytics aggregation pipeline (not nightly pg_cron)
Webhook/integration framework for CRM and help-desk tools (Zendesk, Salesforce, HubSpot) — expected by enterprise buyers
Human handoff/escalation flow implementation (critical for any customer support use case)
GDPR right-to-erasure implementation for vector store (deleting user data from embeddings, not just the document record)
LLM output semantic caching layer
Knowledge base sharing between chatbots within the same organization
Chatbot version rollback mechanism and in-flight conversation handling on version publish
Outbound DPA with LLM sub-processors (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) for the platform's own data flows
security_risks: Widget token stored in localStorage (step 15) is vulnerable to XSS on the host page. Shadow DOM isolation protects styles, not JavaScript. A compromised host page reads localStorage and replays the widget token from any origin.

SSRF via DNS rebinding: An SSRF allowlist checking the IP at request time is bypassable via DNS rebinding (resolve to public IP, allowlisted; then re-resolve to 169.254.169.254 cloud metadata endpoint). Requires DNS pinning or a dedicated egress proxy with IP validation at connection time, not DNS resolution time.

PII in vector embeddings with no deletion guarantee: Step 10 detects PII before storage, but doesn't specify the action (block, redact, anonymize). If a user uploads a document containing customer PII and later requests deletion under GDPR Article 17, soft-deleting the document record does not remove the embeddings. True erasure from HNSW index requires full index rebuild.

LLM Guard regex-based prompt injection is insufficient: Sophisticated jailbreaks use Unicode homoglyphs, prompt continuation attacks, and token manipulation that regex cannot detect. The plan needs adversarial LLM-based input validation (a dedicated classifier or a hardened secondary LLM call) not just LLM Guard.

API keys hashed with bcrypt but bcrypt is not suitable for API key verification: bcrypt is designed to be slow (for passwords). API keys are validated on every request — bcrypt verification at high RPS will become a CPU bottleneck. Use HMAC-SHA256 with a secret pepper for API key hashing/verification.

Multi-tenant isolation relies entirely on application-layer RLS: There is no network-level isolation between tenants. A SQL injection vulnerability anywhere in the backend would bypass RLS and expose all tenant data. Defense-in-depth requires query parameterization enforcement verified by SAST in CI (not just RLS policies).
final_review.summary: This is a well-structured and thorough plan for its scope — the dependency graph is sound, acceptance criteria are mostly concrete, and the technology choices are defensible. However, it has three production-blocking gaps that are not MVP-acceptable: (1) there is no billing or usage metering implementation, meaning the product cannot enforce its own pricing tiers; (2) embedding model lock-in has no resolution path, so users who switch LLM providers will silently get degraded or broken RAG retrieval; and (3) the analytics pipeline's nightly aggregation model contradicts the implied real-time dashboard experience users expect from a product competing with Tidio and Intercom. Security is architecturally mispositioned — bolted on after the backend is built rather than designed alongside the API contract, which will require expensive refactoring. The conversation flow graph schema — the product's core differentiator — is never defined, which means the frontend, API, and database teams have no shared contract for the builder canvas. At 62/100, the plan is a solid foundation that needs four things before development begins: resolve embedding model architecture, add billing/metering as an explicit build step, define the conversation flow schema, and move security design earlier in the dependency chain.

Vectors from different embedding models are incompatible — existing chunks become unqueryable. No re-indexing strategy exists.

pgvector + RLS incompatibility: Step 8 specifies row-level security for multi-tenant isolation, but pgvector's HNSW index scans occur before RLS filtering. This defeats index performance and may leak timing information across tenants. At scale this becomes a correctness AND performance failure.

A SaaS product cannot ship without this.

These are fundamentally different architectures with different cost, margin, compliance, and UI implications. The financial model in step 4 cannot be correct without resolving this.

Prompt injection filtering, input sanitization, and SSRF controls should be designed alongside the API spec (step 7) and implemented during backend construction (step 12), not after. A 50-message conversation cannot be injected wholesale into every LLM call. No truncation, summarization, or sliding window strategy exists anywhere in the plan.

But step 11's acceptance criteria says events are ingested 'within 1s of occurrence.' A user who just deployed a chatbot will see zero analytics until the next morning. Real-time dashboards require a streaming aggregation layer (not pg_cron).

React Flow node schema is never defined: Step 13 calls for a drag-and-drop canvas using React Flow but nowhere in the plan is the conversation flow graph schema defined — what does a node represent? What edges are valid? What is the execution model? This is the core product feature and it has no specification.

Real-time HTTP health pings to OpenAI/Anthropic/Google cannot complete in <10ms. history.summary: This is a well-structured and thorough plan for its scope — the dependency graph is sound, acceptance criteria are mostly concrete, and the technology choices are defensible. However, it has three production-blocking gaps that are not MVP-acceptable: (1) there is no billing or usage metering implementation, meaning the product cannot enforce its own pricing tiers; (2) embedding model lock-in has no resolution path, so users who switch LLM providers will silently get degraded or broken RAG retrieval; and (3) the analytics pipeline's nightly aggregation model contradicts the implied real-time dashboard experience users expect from a product competing with Tidio and Intercom. Security is architecturally mispositioned — bolted on after the backend is built rather than designed alongside the API contract, which will require expensive refactoring. The conversation flow graph schema — the product's core differentiator — is never defined, which means the frontend, API, and database teams have no shared contract for the builder canvas. At 62/100, the plan is a solid foundation that needs four things before development begins: resolve embedding model architecture, add billing/metering as an explicit build step, define the conversation flow schema, and move security design earlier in the dependency chain.
